purpose: Classifies the current cross-asset vol environment to determine positioning approach and risk management framework.
components: Regime A (Low-vol carry): All vol measures suppressed, positive carry environment, diversification works conventionally
Regime B (Synchronized expansion): Vol measures elevated but moving together, traditional correlations hold, systematic risk dominates
Regime C (Divergent vol — current): MOVE elevated, VIX suppressed, FX vol low; driven by Kalecki dual-channel where fiscal spending supports earnings while generating issuance uncertainty
current_assessment: Currently in Regime C (Divergent Vol). MOVE ~100-120, VIX ~13-18, G10 FX vol ~7-9%. Historically unstable with no precedent for persistence as steady state. All three historical analogues (2006-07, 2014, 2017-18) transitioned within 6-18 months.
limitations: Rigid at regime boundaries — transitions may not be cleanly categorized
Only three regimes may oversimplify the state space
Historical analogues have limited sample size
Does not distinguish between benign and violent resolution paths

purpose: Distinguishes between genuinely stable and fragile low-vol environments that produce identical vol surfaces but have radically different risk profiles and transition probabilities.
components: Compute: (Government Deficit/GDP) minus (Change in Private Debt/GDP)
Type A (positive value): Kalecki profit-supported — fiscal flows sustain corporate earnings, genuinely stable low vol
Type B (negative value): Minsky credit-supported — private credit expansion drives earnings and low vol, structurally fragile
Historical calibration: 2010-2013 and 2013-2017 = Type A; 2005-2007 and 2018-2019 = Type B
current_assessment: Current 6-7% fiscal deficit suggests Type A (profit-supported), but simultaneous growth in private credit ($1.7T+) creates mixed signals. The diagnostic may show a transitional state between pure types.
limitations: Tested across only ~3-4 U.S. cycles
Real-time data has 2-3 month publication lag (Federal Reserve Z.1)
Boundary classification involves judgment calls
Unvalidated for non-U.S. economies with different fiscal rules
Needs formal out-of-sample testing

purpose: Identifies the dominant macro stress source and predicts credit market behavior and portfolio correlation dynamics based on VIX-MOVE correlation regime.
components: Regime 1 (Strong positive ~0.65): Demand shock — both vol measures elevated, credit spreads widen fastest, funding channel activated
Regime 2 (Decoupled ~0.25-0.35): Supply/inflation shock — MOVE elevated, VIX suppressed, rates-led stress
Regime 3 (Predicted — VIX up, MOVE down): Fiscal tightening plus levered private sector — early warning signal, untested
Regime 4 (Weakly positive +0.20-0.35 — current): Historically most unstable transitional regime
current_assessment: Currently in the weakly-positive regime (+0.20 to +0.35), historically the most unstable. Specific transition probabilities from this state are unreliable (overfit to n=4 sample). The qualitative insight that this is unstable and transitional is valuable even without precise probabilities.
limitations: Transition probability estimates are overfit (n=4 episodes)
Credit-specific diagnostic use requires additional filters
Regime 3 prediction is entirely untested
Causal chain from macro shock type to correlation regime is clean theoretically but noisy empirically

purpose: Provides real-time observable regime classification for rates vol without the identification lag of formal Markov-switching models.
components: MOVE < 80: Low rates vol regime — expectations-driven yield curve, reliable recession signals, carry strategies favorable
MOVE 80-100: Transitional — elevated uncertainty but not crisis, monitor for direction
MOVE 100-140 (current): High-floor regime — term premium contaminates curve signals, credit conditions tightening at margin, 18+ month persistence
MOVE > 140: Crisis regime — active deleveraging, curve signals dominated by risk premia
current_assessment: MOVE at 100-120 in the high-floor regime for 18+ months, longest since 2008-2012. Four structural drivers (BoJ normalization, Fed terminal rate ambiguity, $2T+ issuance, basis trade fragility) support continued elevation. Question is whether this is the new normal requiring model re-baselining.
limitations: Thresholds are approximate and may need recalibration in structurally higher nominal growth world
Does not capture within-regime dynamics changed by microstructure evolution
Single-dimensional — does not incorporate cross-asset information
Cannot distinguish between different macro causes of elevated MOVE

purpose: Maps Minsky cycle phases to observable credit vol properties for regime identification and timing of credit vol repricing.
components: Hedge phase: Credit vol is mean-reverting, spread shocks quickly absorbed, leverage moderate
Speculative phase: Credit vol becomes path-dependent, spread shocks persist longer, leverage elevated
Ponzi phase: Credit vol becomes explosive, small triggers produce outsized spread moves, leverage extreme
HY-IG spread differential thresholds: <280bp = Hedge/early Speculative; 280-350bp = late Speculative; >350-400bp = approaching Ponzi transition
current_assessment: Current HY-IG spread at ~280-320bp places us in late Speculative phase, below the 350-400bp threshold for serious repositioning. Supporting indicators (leverage ratios up 0.3-0.5x, coverage ratios down 0.5-1.0x, upgrade/downgrade ratio below 1.0 for five quarters) consistent with late-cycle deterioration but not uniquely identifying a regime transition.
limitations: Similar metrics deteriorated in 2015-2016 and 2019 without producing a credit cycle turn
Claimed zero false positives across seven episodes requires independent validation
Single-source framework from credit_analyst_01
Real-time leverage data has reporting lag
Does not incorporate private credit dynamics

purpose: Predicts the order in which vol regime shifts propagate across asset classes to inform hedge timing and cross-asset monitoring.
components: Standard sequence: Rates vol first, then credit vol (1-3 week lag), then equity vol (2-6 week lag), then FX vol
Conditional reliability: ~70% for fundamentally-driven credit-origin transitions; ~40-50% unconditionally
Microstructure disruption: Positioning-driven transitions can skip states entirely (Feb 2018, March 2020)
Private credit opacity disruption: May cause rates to skip credit and propagate directly to FX then equity (no historical analogue)
Monitoring: CLO arbitrage collapse as credit-first signal; October 2023 confirms rates-first propagation
current_assessment: Unconditional 70% reliability was refuted — honest base rate closer to 40-50%. Conditional claim for fundamentally-driven credit-origin transitions survives at moderate confidence. Current monitoring should focus on CLO arbitrage dynamics and rates-to-credit transmission for the next transition. Microstructure-driven skip risk means sequential hedge timing may fail.
limitations: Unconditional base rate inflated by counting partial matches as matches
Timing varies significantly with shock magnitude
Private credit opacity may disrupt sequence in unprecedented ways
Microstructure-driven transitions bypass the framework entirely
Only 5 reference episodes with 2 clean breaks
